title: On its 250th birthday, the US is still defined by its fault lines
gist: On America's 250th anniversary, Chatham House argues the country's defining fault line has shifted from ordinary partisanship to a deeper crisis of legitimacy - collapsing institutional trust, rising political violence, and a fraying two-party structure - raising the once-unthinkable question of whether the political system itself needs to be remade.

insights.claim: The defining fault line in US politics today is not between parties but between those who believe the current system of democracy can deliver rights, equity and prosperity for all, and those who no longer believe it can.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors should treat US political risk as a question of regime legitimacy, not just electoral cycle swings.
insights.quote: The most urgent fault line in US politics is between those who believe the current system of US democracy can provide rights, equity and prosperity for all, and those who do not.

insights.claim: US history can be read as a repeating cycle of 'rupture and repair' across fault lines - independence from Britain, the Civil War over slavery, farm-versus-city tension from industrialization, the 1960s-70s social revolutions, and recurring economic-distribution disputes from taxation without representation to Occupy Wall Street - implying the current moment is another such rupture point.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frames current US instability as structurally recurrent rather than a one-off aberration, useful for calibrating how long the volatility might persist.
insights.locator: history of rupture and repair section

insights.claim: Americans are now openly asking whether it is necessary to again 'dissolve the political bands' that connect them, echoing the language of the Declaration of Independence but applied internally rather than against a foreign power.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals elevated tail risk of secessionist or system-rejecting rhetoric moving from fringe to mainstream discourse.
insights.quote: A long unthinkable question is being asked by Americans: is it necessary again to dissolve the political bands which connect them?

insights.claim: US public trust in government has collapsed from 49% at the turn of the century to just 18% as of 2025.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A trust baseline this low raises the bar for any policy or institution to be seen as legitimate, increasing volatility around contested outcomes (elections, court rulings).

insights.claim: Supreme Court favourability has reached historic lows even as Americans increasingly believe their own freedoms are under threat.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A judiciary with cratering public confidence weakens its ability to serve as a legitimacy backstop in future contested disputes (elections, executive power fights).

insights.claim: The Supreme Court's recent rulings sent mixed signals - easing campaign finance caps, narrowing the Voting Rights Act's scope, and affirming presidential power to remove independent agency members, while also upholding mail-in ballot rules and birthright citizenship - leaving no clear ideological throughline for Americans seeking clarity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Ambiguous jurisprudence makes it harder for markets to price regulatory and electoral-law risk consistently going into the midterms.

insights.claim: A record 45% of Americans now identify as political independents, alongside a declining share strongly identifying as either Republican or Democrat, showing the two-party system is cracking under the legitimacy fault line.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Rising independent identification increases the odds of outsider or third-force candidates disrupting the 2026 midterms and beyond.

insights.claim: US political conflict has shifted over the past decade from partisanship and polarization toward radicalization, marked by the January 6, 2021 Capitol takeover to reject the election outcome.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Marks a qualitative escalation in US political risk that goes beyond normal partisan cycling and toward legitimacy-contesting violence.

insights.claim: Politically motivated attacks have surged in the US: Paul Pelosi was attacked in his home in 2022, Donald Trump faced assassination attempts in both 2024 and 2026, and in 2025 Minnesota Democratic state representative Melissa Hortman and her husband, as well as right-wing activist Charlie Kirk, were assassinated.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A pattern of assassinations and attempts across both parties signals systemic political violence risk relevant to security, insurance and event-risk pricing around US political figures.

insights.claim: US government departments have flagged the 'accelerationist' movement - the ideology that the democratic state is so corrupt and irreparable that violent action must be taken to precipitate its replacement - as a growing domestic threat.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Formal government threat-flagging of an anti-system ideology suggests domestic extremism risk is being institutionally tracked, not just anecdotal.

insights.claim: As many as a third of polled Americans agree the US government is corrupt and that it may soon be time to take up arms against it, indicating a meaningful minority is shifting from 'bullets over ballots' rhetoric toward literal endorsement of armed resistance.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A poll this stark on armed resistance sentiment is a leading indicator for civil-unrest and political-violence tail risk that isn't yet reflected in mainstream risk pricing.
insights.quote: Americans are increasingly considering bullets over ballots.

insights.claim: New York City mayor and self-described democratic socialist Zohran Mamdani's rise exemplifies the fault line, alongside outsider political voices performing well in primaries ahead of the 2026 midterms, showing an appetite for new leadership outside the establishment.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Outsider/insurgent candidates succeeding in primaries is a signal to watch for further disruption of establishment incumbents through the 2026 midterms.

insights.claim: A majority of Americans now view billionaires as a threat to democracy amid a broader demand for affordability, reflecting a shift in views of wealth.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Rising anti-billionaire sentiment is a political tailwind for wealth-tax and antitrust policy proposals investors should track.

insights.claim: Growing skepticism toward AI - now the engine of US economic growth - is visible in polling: more Americans think AI will have a negative impact on society and are concerned about the personal dangers it poses.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Rising public AI skepticism creates political tailwind for AI regulation even as AI investment drives a large share of US GDP and equity market growth.

insights.claim: Both major US parties are feeling the heat of the legitimacy fault line: for Republicans, party cohesion has for a decade been anchored by Trump and the MAGA movement, but defections from prominent figures like Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene and journalist Tucker Carlson reveal cracks.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Defections from high-profile MAGA-aligned figures suggest the Republican coalition's cohesion cannot be assumed stable into the midterms.
